essential_question: Why must vertical angles always be congruent — not just usually equal?
objective: Justify why vertical angles are always congruent (both are supplements of the same angle), review the week's Math-U-See practice, and pass the mastery checkpoint on adjacent, complementary, supplementary, and vertical angles.

content.prompt: Without looking, sketch two intersecting lines, label the four angles ∠1–∠4 in order, and mark the two vertical pairs. Then say from memory what makes angles adjacent, complementary, supplementary, and vertical.

content.heading: Go over Lesson Practice 6A & 6B together
content.note: Bring your completed MUS Lesson Practice 6A/6B. We check the tricky ones: reading vertical vs. adjacent off a busy diagram, telling complementary (90°) from supplementary (180°) at a glance, and the Greek-letter angle names some problems use (α, β, γ, δ). Fix any you missed and note WHY.

content.heading: Defend three claims
bullets: Claim 1: vertical angles are always congruent. Argue it — ∠1 and ∠3 are each a linear pair with the SAME angle ∠2, so both must equal 180° minus m∠2.
Claim 2: adjacent angles do NOT have to be complementary or supplementary. Argue it with a counterexample — two adjacent angles measuring, say, 20° and 100°.

Claim 3: a linear pair is always supplementary, but two supplementary angles are not always a linear pair. Argue it — a 50° angle and a 130° angle drawn nowhere near each other are still supplementary, but they share no vertex and no side.

content.preview: Next week, Lesson 7 — Transversals & Parallel Lines — where these same angle pairs reappear when one line crosses two parallel lines.

success_criteria.text: I can identify and symbolize adjacent, complementary, supplementary, and vertical angles without help
I can compute an unknown angle's measure given its complementary, supplementary, or vertical partner
I can justify why vertical angles are always congruent, using the fact that both are supplements of the same angle

questions.prompt: Two angles that share a vertex and a side, without overlapping, are called…
questions.explain: Adjacent angles share a vertex and one ray, sitting right next to each other with no overlap.

questions.prompt: Two angles whose measures add to 180° are called ___ angles.
questions.explain: Supplementary angles' measures always add to exactly 180° — enough to fill a straight line.

questions.prompt: ∠A and ∠B are complementary. If m∠A = 37°, what is m∠B?
Two lines intersect at H, forming ∠1, ∠2, ∠3, ∠4 in order around H. If m∠2 = 48°, find m∠1 and m∠3, and name the relationship you used for each.
questions.answer: m∠1 = 132° (180° − 48°, since ∠1 and ∠2 are a linear pair — supplementary); m∠3 = 48° (∠2 and ∠3 are vertical angles, always congruent).
questions.explain: ∠1 and ∠2 are adjacent and form a straight line (linear pair, supplementary); ∠2 and ∠3 are opposite each other across the vertex (vertical, congruent).

questions.prompt: Draw two lines crossing at a point V. Label the four angles ∠1, ∠2, ∠3, ∠4 in order, mark ONE vertical pair with matching arc marks, and mark ONE linear pair showing their measures sum to 180°.
questions.answer: Two lines crossing at V with four angles labeled in order; one vertical pair marked with matching arcs, and one adjacent (linear) pair marked as summing to 180°.
questions.explain: Rubric: two crossing lines with four labeled angles in order (1 pt) + a correctly marked vertical pair AND a correctly marked linear pair (1 pt).

questions.prompt: In your own words, explain why vertical angles must always be congruent — use the word 'supplement' in your answer.
questions.answer: Each vertical angle forms a linear pair (a supplement) with the same third angle between them; since both vertical angles are supplements of that identical angle, 180° minus the same number gives the same result twice, so the two vertical angles must be equal.
questions.explain: Full credit: names that both vertical angles are supplements of the SAME adjacent angle, which forces their measures to match.

questions.prompt: Close: rate yourself on Lesson 6 (Emerging / Proficient / Mastered) and name the one idea you'd want to revisit.
